content:
Hi all,
I previously worked on a pointer formatting patch for the kernel fmt module (last submitted version: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20260311-hashedptr-v11-1-dd1b8e8b0fb0@kylinos.cn/).

I learned a lot from all the valuable feedback everyone gave on earlier versions. I've made another round of improvements recently, and I'd like to get some early review suggestions before submitting the next formal version.

The latest version ready for review is at:
https://github.com/alvinsun039/linux-torvalds/commit/eee0c7e107042a98914b47a6f0d99fd05145f5ac

Key changes:

  • Add T: ?Sized bound to all Pointer trait implementations, combined with standard cast::<()>() type conversion in HashedPtr to uniformly extract pointer data address and automatically discard metadata, adding support for {:p} formatting of all fat pointer types
  • Add Pointer trait implementation for mutable references
  • Add zero-padding format support
  • Refactor test suite: add RAII state guard, split into independent test cases, add fat pointer equality validation tests
    Any comments, suggestions or review feedback are highly appreciated!

Thanks,
Alvin Sun
I think this would be a lot easier to read if you could expand the uses of Pointer::fmt into <YourTypeGoesHere as Pointer>::fmt

Great call, thanks! The code reads much clearer now. Any thoughts on the implementation?

Alice Ryhl said:

I think this would be a lot easier to read if you could expand the uses of Pointer::fmt into <YourTypeGoesHere as Pointer>::fmt
What I'd want to see is the compiler errors that result from various methods of printing a pointer.
I cannot really judge what they will be from the implementation.
What you mean is that you don't want printing pointers in the kernel, even if it's just the hash of the pointer?
Let's say I do pr_warn("{:?}", my_raw_ptr), then what happens?
It's pretty hard to get rid of that debug impl
(I personally also don't think having Debug printing the raw pointer being too big of a problem)
@Gary Guo Do you think we need to consider fat pointers, or is it sufficient to only handle thin pointers?

The original motivation for submitting this patch was to address the issue where impl_fmt_adapter_forward! forwarding was printing the stack address of the pointer variable itself, rather than the value of the pointer.

For that motivation, this would be the most straightforward fix?

diff --git a/rust/kernel/fmt.rs b/rust/kernel/fmt.rs
index 1e8725eb44ed..61542a0e3341 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/fmt.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/fmt.rs
@@ -17,10 +17,9 @@
 macro_rules! impl_fmt_adapter_forward {
     ($($trait:ident),* $(,)?) => {
         $(
-            impl<T: $trait> $trait for Adapter<T> {
+            impl<T: $trait> $trait for Adapter<&T> {
                 fn fmt(&self, f: &mut Formatter<'_>) -> Result {
-                    let Self(t) = self;
-                    $trait::fmt(t, f)
+                    <T as $trait>::fmt(self.0, f)
                 }
             }
         )*
Alice previously reminded that directly printing kernel pointers is unsafe, so we still need to implement pointer hashing functionality through scnprintf("%p", ...).
I think these are really independent works? I don't think printing out actual addressses unhashed is worse than printing out wrong stack addresses unhashed
So these are two separate patches: first fix pointer printing, then add hash ptr support? For custom Pointer trait implementations, should we keep it simple and only consider thin pointers?
Or do you have any better suggestions?

I think an earlier version uses hash and defer to Rust pointer fmt approach, that approach works very well with fat pointers. With the current approach I think you cannot support fat pointers without unstable features like ptr_metadata.
I'm converting fat pointers to thin pointers inside HashedPtr using let ptr = self.0.cast::<()>().
